title: Charmed Life - Chapter 12 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter 12

12

C
 HRESTOMANCI’S STUDY was a large, sun-filled room with books in shelves all around it. There was a desk, but Chrestomanci was not sitting at it. He was sprawled on a sofa in the sun, reading a newspaper and wearing a green dressing gown with golden dragons on it. The gold embroidery of the dragons winked and glittered in the sun. Cat could not take his eyes off them. He stood just inside the door, not daring to go any farther, and he thought: He has found out about the dragons’ blood.

Chrestomanci looked up and smiled. “Don’t look so frightened,” he said, laying down his newspaper. “Come and sit down.”

He pointed to a large leather armchair. It was all in his friendliest way but, these days, Cat was sure this meant precisely nothing. He was sure that the friendlier Chrestomanci seemed, the angrier this meant he was. He stole over to the armchair and sat in it. It proved to be one of those deep, sloping kind of chairs. Cat slid backwards down the slippery leather slope of its seat until he found he was having to look at Chrestomanci from between his knees. He felt quite defenseless. He thought he ought to say something, so he whispered, “Good morning.”

“You don’t look as if you thought so,” observed Chrestomanci. “No doubt you have your reasons. But don’t worry. This isn’t exactly about the frog again. You see, I’ve been thinking about you—”

“Oh, you needn’t!” Cat said from his half-lying position. He felt that if Chrestomanci were to fix his thoughts on something on the other side of the universe, it would hardly be too far away.

“It didn’t hurt much,” said Chrestomanci. “Thank you all the same. As I was saying, the frog affair set me thinking. And though I fear you probably have as little moral sense as your wretched sister, I wondered if I could trust you. Do you think I can trust you?”

Cat had no idea where this could be leading, except that from the way Chrestomanci put it, he did not seem to trust Cat very much. “Nobody’s ever trusted me before,” Cat said cautiously—except Janet, he thought, and only because she had no choice.

“But it might be worth trying, don’t you think?” suggested Chrestomanci. “I ask because I’m going to start you on witchcraft lessons.”

Cat had simply not expected this. He was horrified. His legs waved about in the chair with the shock. He managed to stop them, but he was still horrified. The moment Mr. Saunders started trying to teach him magic, it would be obvious that Cat had no witchcraft at all. Then Chrestomanci would start to think about the frog all over again. Cat cursed the chance that had made Janet draw in her breath and caused him to confess. “Oh, you mustn’t do that!” he said. “It would be quite fatal. I mean, you can’t trust me at all. I’m black-hearted. I’m evil. It was living with Mrs. Sharp that did it. If I learned witchcraft, there’s no knowing what I’d do. Look what I did to Euphemia.”

“That,” said Chrestomanci, “is just the kind of accident I’m anxious to prevent. If you learn how and what to do, you’re far less likely to make that kind of mistake again.”

“Yes, but I’d probably do it on purpose,” Cat assured him. “You’ll be putting the means in my hands.”

“You have it there anyway,” Chrestomanci said. “And witchcraft will out, you know. No one who has it can resist using it forever. What exactly makes you think you’re so wicked?”

vocabulary.definition: An unplanned event that causes harm or damage.
vocabulary.example: That is just the kind of accident I’m anxious to prevent.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing strong displeasure or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: He was sure that the friendlier Chrestomanci seemed, the angrier this meant he was.

vocabulary.definition: Experiencing worry or unease about something.
vocabulary.example: That is just the kind of accident I’m anxious to prevent.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that deliberately avoids potential problems or dangers.
vocabulary.example: 'Nobody’s ever trusted me before,' Cat said cautiously.

vocabulary.definition: The bone that connects the breastbone to the shoulder blade.
vocabulary.example: He rested his hand on his collarbone.

vocabulary.definition: A habitual combination of words that is characteristic of a particular language.
vocabulary.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: To admit or acknowledge something reluctantly or formally.
vocabulary.example: ...and caused him to confess.

vocabulary.definition: To invoke harm or evil upon someone or something.
vocabulary.example: Cat cursed the chance that had made Janet draw in her breath.

vocabulary.definition: Not protected against harm or attack.
vocabulary.example: He felt quite defenseless.

vocabulary.definition: Decorative needlework on fabric.
vocabulary.example: The gold embroidery of the dragons winked and glittered in the sun.

vocabulary.definition: Most kind and pleasant.
vocabulary.example: It was all in his friendliest way but, these days, Cat was sure this meant precisely nothing.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of being friendly.
vocabulary.example: It was all in his friendliest way but, these days, Cat was sure this meant precisely nothing.

vocabulary.definition: To shine with a bright, shimmering light.
vocabulary.example: The gold embroidery of the dragons winked and glittered in the sun.

vocabulary.definition: Filled with horror; shocked and upset.
vocabulary.example: Cat was horrified.

vocabulary.definition: A material made from animal skin, used for making furniture, clothes, and accessories.
vocabulary.example: He slid backwards down the slippery leather slope of its seat.

vocabulary.definition: A period of teaching or learning.
vocabulary.example: I’m going to start you on witchcraft lessons.

vocabulary.definition: A detailed description of the course of instruction for one lesson.
vocabulary.example: They discussed the lesson plan carefully.

vocabulary.definition: A phrase consisting of multiple words that function as a single unit of meaning.
vocabulary.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: Exactly or sharply defined or stated.
vocabulary.example: He was sure this meant precisely nothing.

vocabulary.definition: To withstand the action or effect of something.
vocabulary.example: No one who has it can resist using it forever.

vocabulary.definition: To move smoothly over a surface.
vocabulary.example: He slid backwards down the slippery leather slope.

vocabulary.definition: To move smoothly, sliding accidentally.
vocabulary.example: Cat slid backwards down the slippery leather slope of its seat.

vocabulary.definition: To sit or lie with arms and legs spread out carelessly or awkwardly.
vocabulary.example: He was sprawled on a sofa in the sun, reading a newspaper.

vocabulary.definition: A thing that prompts or encourages someone to do something.
vocabulary.example: The frog affair set me thinking.

vocabulary.definition: Evil or morally bad.
vocabulary.example: What exactly makes you think you’re so wicked?

ru.translation: нанёс (возмездие, ущерб) [прим: глагол 'wreak' употребляется в выражениях]

vocabulary.definition: Cause or inflict (something, typically something harmful).
vocabulary.example: He feared the havoc he might wreak.

vocabulary.definition: Very bad or unpleasant; miserable.
vocabulary.example: Though I fear you probably have as little moral sense as your wretched sister.
